title: Graph Theory, Networks, Scheduling, and Routing · Scenario Checkpoint
summary: Design routes and schedules for deliveries and shared services. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a network routing and scheduling analysis. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Represent vertices, edges, direction, weights, and attributes from a real system.
Analyze paths, cycles, components, reachability, and bottlenecks.
Use trees and spanning structures for hierarchy and low-cost connectivity.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.python:
"""applied-mathematics-intermediate cumulative model portfolio."""

from math import exp, log, sqrt


def reasonableness(value: float, lower: float, upper: float) -> bool:
    return lower <= value <= upper

# Add one tested model increment per activity.

steps.title: Reconstruct the defensible process
steps.instruction: Order the Graph Representation evidence cycle before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Graph Representation assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: Represent vertices, edges, direction, weights, and attributes from a real system. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

steps.title: Calculate the unfamiliar quantity
steps.instruction: A model passes through (4, 14) and (8, 26). Calculate its average rate of change.
steps.why: A numerical result is not complete until its unit, assumptions, scale, and tolerance survive a reasonableness check.

content.text: Use Δoutput/Δinput = (26 − 14) / (8 − 4).

content.title: Before calculating
content.text: Predict the sign and approximate magnitude. Enter the requested quantity with a unit, then compare it with that prediction.

hints: Use Δoutput/Δinput = (26 − 14) / (8 − 4).
Keep full working precision and round only the final reported value.
The accepted result is within 0.001 of the exact value and includes units per input or per input.

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write a concise evidence note that explains when Network Tradeoffs applies, what can fail, and how the changed case was verified.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Compare efficiency, resilience, fairness, and capacity in network decisions.

checks.description: The evidence cycle runs from problem framing through changed-case validation.
checks.failureMessage: Frame the decision before modeling, and validate after obtaining a result.

checks.description: The decision applies Graph Representation to the changed stakeholder case.
checks.failureMessage: Recheck the conditions and reject the documented misconception about Graph Representation.

checks.description: The decision applies Network Tradeoffs to the changed stakeholder case.
checks.failureMessage: Recheck the conditions and reject the documented misconception about Network Tradeoffs.

checks.description: The calculated quantity is accurate to the stated tolerance and carries its requested unit.
checks.failureMessage: Recalculate from the documented model, check the scale, and include the requested unit.

checks.description: The evidence note explains the model, failure risk, and verification in the learner’s own words.
checks.failureMessage: Add a causal explanation using compare and efficiency plus specific case evidence.
